Tennis Automated | ATP Fantasy | Week 4 Results

Week 4 Results Cheat Sheet

Results edition for 20-26 April 2026. Week 4 remained centered on the Madrid ATP Masters 1000, with the official event window running 22 April - 3 May, 2026. With the week now complete from a fantasy standpoint, the focus shifts from draw theory to who actually converted, which player types paid off in Madrid conditions, and what the finished scoring environment means going forward.

Week Window 20-26 April
Slate Madrid only
Surface Clay altitude
Status Results edition

Week 4 Takeaways

What Paid Off

Madrid again rewarded fantasy builds that leaned into premium talent, clean advancement paths, and players able to protect serve in quicker clay conditions. The biggest winners were the roster spots that turned theoretical fit into actual match wins, especially players who combined progression with efficient scoring patterns.

  • Deep-run equity mattered more than name value alone.
  • Serve-driven scoring remained highly relevant in altitude.
  • Straight-sets progress was a major separator.
  • Elite anchors justified their salary when they avoided early turbulence.

What Hurt

The misses were familiar: expensive names who failed to convert their section, trendy upside plays who ran into early resistance, and lineups that overcommitted to crowded pockets of the draw. In a one-event fantasy week, one early exit from a premium slot could swing the entire slate.

  • Popular picks with difficult paths became costly quickly.
  • Volatile value plays were harder to trust without advancement.
  • Overstacking one section increased downside.
  • Bonus Ball misses were especially damaging on a Madrid-only slate.

What Mattered In The Results

ATP Fantasy still ran through the familiar structure of a 100-credit budget, six starters, two alternates, and one Bonus Ball player scoring double. In a standalone Masters 1000 week, completed results reinforced the same core lesson: advancement remains the cleanest path to a strong total, but in Madrid, efficient wins can widen the gap fast.

The finished week also underlined that Madrid is not just generic clay. Players who could hold serve, shorten points, and avoid messy early-round battles gave fantasy managers the best return. The lineups that scored best generally paired one or two elite anchors with mid-tier names who actually converted their section rather than simply projecting well on paper.

+2 per ace +10 straight-sets win +20 upset win -2 per double fault -20 upset loss
Results Edition Themes
  • Completed wins mattered more than pre-week draw optimism.
  • Bonus Ball success was tied to reliable advancement, not just ceiling.
  • Madrid-specific power translated when backed by a manageable route.
  • Misses usually came from early exits in premium roster spots.

Fantasy Lessons From The Week

Best roster construction lesson

Balanced builds still worked, but only when the cheaper pieces had real path-to-points potential. Saving credits without securing advancement rarely paid off.

Best scoring lesson

Aces, holds, and straight-sets wins remained meaningful edges in Madrid conditions. Players who won cleanly outperformed grinders who needed long, volatile matches.

Best process takeaway

The strongest pre-lock reads were still the ones built around section clarity and conversion probability. Madrid rewarded players who could turn favorable conditions into actual progression.

Best caution flag

Results again showed that popularity is not protection. Expensive or trendy names can still become lineup traps if they fail to survive the early rounds.

Top Performer Archetypes

Elite title threats

The safest fantasy returns came from top-end players who matched ranking strength with actual week-long stability. When elite players advanced cleanly, they remained the foundation of winning builds.

Serve-first clay attackers

Madrid continued to reward players whose serve and first-strike patterns translated to altitude clay. These profiles often created stronger ace totals and cleaner set margins than slower-clay specialists.

Mid-tier section converters

Some of the best value came from players outside the very top tier who simply took advantage of a playable section. In a one-event slate, reaching one extra round can be the difference between average and excellent value.

Bonus Ball Review

Best Bonus Ball profile

The strongest Bonus Ball outcomes came from players with both title-level upside and a stable route through the scoring window. Reliability mattered as much as raw ceiling.

What worked

Using the multiplier on a premium favorite with strong advancement equity remained the cleanest strategy. Madrid still rewarded backing players who could stack wins rather than chase one-off volatility.

What missed

Bonus Ball choices became costly when managers chased matchup-specific upside without enough path security. On a single-event week, a short run from your multiplier spot was hard to recover from.

Outperformers

Premium anchors who delivered

The best-performing expensive picks were the ones who justified their salary with progression and efficient match scoring. In Madrid, paying up worked when those stars actually converted their draw position.

Undervalued power profiles

Players priced below the elite tier but suited to altitude clay often returned strong value if they found early rhythm. This was the clearest outperformer lane of the week.

Playable-section risers

Managers who identified open pockets of the draw were often rewarded. A manageable route remained one of the strongest predictors of fantasy overperformance.

Alternates with real upside

Alternate spots were most useful when they covered volatile but live names in favorable sections. Even without starring in every lineup, that flexibility mattered.

Misses And Traps

Early-exit premium names

The biggest fantasy misses were expensive players who failed to turn reputation into advancement. On a Madrid-only slate, those losses were difficult to offset elsewhere.

Crowded-quarter gambles

Backing too many players from one dangerous section often backfired. Even strong fits can disappoint when the route is packed with live opposition.

Slow-build clay specialists

Players who rely on long rallies and attritional scoring were less reliable if they could not control matches in these conditions. Madrid still favored cleaner first-strike tennis.

Results edition updated for the completed fantasy week of 20-26 April 2026 using official ATP event timing and ATP scores/results context for Madrid. Use this recap to refine future decisions around advancement equity, Bonus Ball stability, and which clay-power profiles actually convert in Madrid conditions.
